claims.claim: Post-trained NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Ultra reached 5.8% all-pass on a held-out subset of Harvey's Legal Agent Benchmark, from 0% at baseline, landing between Sonnet 4.6 (4.2%) and Opus 4.6 (6.6%).
On the rubric-criteria pass-rate metric, post-trained Nemotron 3 Ultra reached 83% (from 52% base), versus GPT 5.5 at 78% and Sonnet 4.6 / Opus 4.6 both at 85%.

Harvey's published full LAB leaderboard (a different, larger task set than the Nemotron held-out subset) lists Claude Opus 4.7 at 7.1%, Sonnet 4.6 at 5.4%, Opus 4.6 at 4.2%, GPT-5.5 at 2.1%, and Gemini 3.5 Flash at 0.8% all-pass.
Under Harvey's strict all-pass standard, frontier models complete less than 10% of LAB tasks end-to-end, and topping the leaderboard costs roughly $50 per task and over 20 minutes of latency.
Harvey's LAB v1 comprises more than 1,200 agent tasks across 24 legal practice areas, evaluated by over 75,000 expert-written rubric criteria, derived from real client matters.
Nemotron 3 Ultra is a 550B-total / 55B-active-parameter hybrid Mamba-2 + MoE + attention model with up to 1M-token context, released June 4, 2026 under the permissive OpenMDW-1.1 license.

Trajectory says it post-trained Nemotron 3 Ultra on the Harvey LAB in under 24 hours by reusing the same harness, data, and recipe it had used for Nemotron 3 Super, without disclosing the training method.
Trajectory claims its post-trained Nemotron runs at least 10x cheaper than leading closed models; the Harvey tweet states 1/8th to 1/50th the per-token price of Sonnet 4.6 and Opus 4.6.

Independent third-party serverless pricing for Nemotron 3 Ultra runs roughly $0.37-0.50 per million input tokens and $1.08-2.50 output, versus Sonnet 4.6 at $3/$15 and Opus 4.6 at $5/$25, yielding a verified per-token gap of about 1/6 to 1/23, not 1/50.
Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards lifted DeepSeek-R1-Zero from 15.6% to 71.0% pass@1 on AIME 2024, establishing that rubric/verifiable-reward RL can produce large reasoning gains.

Research shows RL-with-verifiable-rewards often does not expand a model's reasoning beyond the base model: base models match or exceed RL-trained variants at large pass@k, implying RL reweights existing paths rather than teaching new capability.

RLVR gains can be artifacts: random or correctness-uncorrelated rewards lifted Qwen2.5-Math-7B by 21.4 points on MATH-500, nearly matching the 29.1-point ground-truth gain, via a GRPO optimization bias.
Harvey raised $200M in March 2026 at an $11B valuation, serving 1,300+ organizations and 100,000+ lawyers, with roughly $300M ARR, and does not build its own frontier model.
NVIDIA positions open models as ecosystem infrastructure that runs on its stack, with Jensen Huang saying the AI ecosystem needs open models and open stacks.

Open-weight models reached parity with closed frontier on code and math by H1 2026 but still trailed materially on factual retrieval (SimpleQA gap ~17.7 points) and 1M long-context (MRCR gap ~9.4 points) - the dimensions most relevant to legal document work.

Harvey's LAB is post-trained by multiple independent vendors with different recipes (Trajectory on Nemotron; Baseten Research using Iterative SFT + GRPO on Qwen3.5), so results from one vendor must not be attributed to another.
